description: Machine-readable source of truth for Alcheris interactive blocks, mobile behavior, and learning beats. Human docs (references/*.md) and the native in-app agent prompt both derive from this file. Shapes verified against the frontend renderer/widget code.

pedagogical_spine.hook: The engagement opener: 'why should I care?' - a relatable scene, stakes, a curiosity gap, or a 'by the end you'll...' promise, in persona. Distinct from realize; earns attention before any content. Usually an article opener with one inline interactive pull.
pedagogical_spine.realize: The learner notices the phenomenon via an interactive element (drag a slider, watch an animation, notice prompt) BEFORE any rule is stated.
pedagogical_spine.understand: Give the rule, model, and worked example; scannable - bold key terms, callouts for the core rule/trap, bullets or an accordion for multi-point content, with inline widgets.
pedagogical_spine.apply: Guided practice (varied types) + real production. Light checks stay INLINE in the article; a sustained production task (essay/coding/exam) becomes a split pinned workspace.
pedagogical_spine.reflect: A short article closer in persona: what you can now do, the one big idea, a next step.
pedagogical_spine.format: Teaching flows as INTERACTIVE ARTICLES (single column, interactions and quick checks woven inline - the explorable-explanation format). Use SPLIT/pinned-workspace layout only when a sustained task needs guidance kept beside it (essay/coding/data-lab/exam). Do not split-ify ordinary teaching.
pedagogical_spine.rule: Teach before you test: never place a question/task about a rule before it is hooked, realized, and understood; never open a page with a wall of text.

authoring_rules.emphasis: Bold key terms; put core rules, traps, and warnings in callouts. Do not bold whole sentences, over-highlight, or use ALL CAPS.
authoring_rules.multi_point: Explanations covering three or more parallel points, rules, items, or steps use a bullet list or an accordion, not a dense paragraph. Text blocks support bullet lists and markdown bold.
authoring_rules.exercise_variety: Do not make every exercise multiple choice. Mix quiz types short_answer / multiple_select / cloze and standalone sequence / cloze / flashcard / comparison blocks, matched to the skill (recognition->quiz, ordering->sequence, precise wording->cloze, recall->flashcard, weak-vs-strong->comparison).
authoring_rules.answer_distribution: Vary the correct-answer position in multiple choice. NEVER default the correct option to A (correctAnswerIndex 0). Order options naturally, not answer-first, and distribute correct answers across A/B/C/D roughly evenly across the lesson. Every-answer-is-A is a broken lesson.
authoring_rules.no_duplicate_practice: Do not duplicate practice across pages. Each page's exercises must be distinct in content and type; never repeat the same question, reuse a block type with the same material, or give two pages an identical practice structure.
authoring_rules.production: Every taught skill needs at least one task where the learner PRODUCES it (short_answer for a sentence, essay for longer writing, custom_activity for structured tasks). For language/writing, test USING the language, e.g. 'write one sentence describing this trend using a strong verb', not only choosing a definition.
authoring_rules.vocabulary: Do not dump vocabulary or long reference lists into one flat table. Use flashcard decks grouped by meaning, grouped accordions, or compact grouped lists, and pair a vocabulary bank with at least one usage exercise.
authoring_rules.illustration_placement: An illustration always goes in the RIGHT panel using illustration mode as the single right-panel block; it demands space.
authoring_rules.persona: Every lesson has a teaching persona (voice); write ALL learner-facing text in it consistently (hook, explanations, feedback, callouts). Teachers pick a preset or define their own; if the user names a character/vibe, build a custom persona from it. Voice never changes the pedagogy, only how it sounds, and it NEVER shortens the teaching - keep full explanatory depth, at least one worked example, and the why (not just the what). Punchy personas (Dante/high-energy) must be cool AND thorough, not reduce explanations to one-liners. Keep it classroom-appropriate. See references/alcheris-teacher-personas.md.
authoring_rules.interactive_article: Single-column pages are interactive articles: weave interactions and quick checks INLINE into the narrative (drag-to-reveal graph, animation, inline quiz/cloze, try-this widget). Make this the default for teaching; reserve split layout for sustained workspaces (essay/coding/data-lab/exam).
authoring_rules.engagement_hook: Open every lesson with a why-should-I-care hook (relatable scene, stakes, curiosity gap, 'by the end you'll...'), in persona, with one inline interactive pull - before any content.
authoring_rules.progressive_disclosure: Keep the main path clean; put depth (the why, proofs, edge cases, history, fast-finisher challenges) in 'go deeper' accordions and side callouts, not the main flow.

engagement_patterns: Reveal before explain: let the learner uncover the trend or curve first, then teach it.
Manipulate before tell: let the learner move a slider and form a hypothesis before stating the rule.
Watch before summarize: play the transformation, then state the takeaway.

interactive_first_rules.signal: a function or 'what if we change X'
a process or transformation to watch
a before/after change is the point
a diagram built step by step

media_in_practice_blocks.note: Quiz questions and flashcards accept images, but agents usually omit them and ship text-only. Include an image whenever a visual aids the question or the recall - especially chart/diagram questions and vocabulary/object flashcards. Reuse an existing lesson image URL or an uploaded asset URL.
media_in_practice_blocks.quiz_question_image: Each object in content.questions may include image: '<url>', rendered above the question. Use it when the question refers to a chart, diagram, map, or picture.
media_in_practice_blocks.flashcard_card_image: Each object in content.cards may include image: '<url>' and imagePosition: 0-100 (vertical focal point, default 50). Use images for vocabulary, objects, places, and visual recall.

graph.purpose: Animated line graph; a slider progressively reveals data points so the learner uncovers the trend.
graph.prefer_over: A static image chart whenever data changes across time and the learner should notice the movement.

content_schema.showFullGraph: boolean (true shows the whole line at once; use for reference/model pages)
content_schema.graphData: array of { id:string, label:string (x tick), value:number (y), annotation?:string } for a single line
content_schema.series: OPTIONAL alternative to graphData for MULTIPLE lines: array of { label:string, color?:hex, points:[{label,value,annotation?}] } - drawn with a legend and shared slider reveal

cautions: Do not put the answer in annotation on a point shown before a quiz about it.

distribution.purpose: Live normal-distribution curve reshaped by mean and standard-deviation sliders.
distribution.prefer_over: A static curve image plus prose when teaching spread, averages, or variance.

content_schema.allowStudentInteraction: boolean (false locks the sliders to teacher-set values)

example.statInstruction: Drag the sliders to see how mean and standard deviation reshape the data.

equation.purpose: Function grapher from an editable expression with variable sliders; gameMode makes a match-the-curve puzzle.
equation.prefer_over: A static formula and single example when teaching functions, transformations, or parameters.

content_schema.targets: array of { x:number, y:number } (puzzle target points when gameMode is true)

embed.purpose: External interactive web resource inside the interaction frame.

illustration.purpose: Keyframed multi-scene visual explanation; scenes auto-play and auto-advance. Use when a process or transformation is best shown in motion.
illustration.prefer_over: A bullet list of steps when motion or staged reveal is the teaching object.

content_schema.scenes: array of { id, title, durationMs, layers:[layer], keyframes:{ [layerId]: [keyframe] } }
content_schema.layer: { id, type:'shape|text|image|svg', x,y,width,height, and type-specific: shape('shape' e.g. rect), text('text'), image/svg('src'); style: opacity,rotation,scaleX,scaleY,fill,stroke,color,fontSize,borderRadius }

cautions: Verify no text/visual overlap in the player.

comparison.purpose: Before/after: image slider, text word-diff, or code line-diff.
comparison.prefer_over: Two stacked images or blocks when the change itself is the lesson.

content_schema.image: beforeImage, afterImage (urls); optional beforeAnnotations/afterAnnotations = { markers:[], drawings:[] }

canvas.purpose: Replayable drawing; plays back paths like a hand drawing live, or a student sketch surface.
canvas.prefer_over: One finished diagram image when the build order teaches something.

content_schema.paths: array (stroke data authored in the visual editor)

mindmap.purpose: Node/relationship map for concept maps, brainstorms, and relationship structures.

content_schema.nodes: array of { id:string, x:number, y:number, label:string, type?:'root', color?:hex }

chart.purpose: Responsive data chart (line, bar, area, pie) with multiple series and optionally multiple charts per block. Use to PRESENT data figures. Use interaction.graph instead when you want a drag-to-reveal exploration; use chart when you just need to show the data (especially multi-series, which interaction.graph also supports but chart renders more richly).

content_schema.title: string
content_schema.xKey: field name in each data row for the x-axis/category (default 'label'; pie uses 'name')
content_schema.series: array of { key:string (a field in the data rows), label?:string, color?:hex }
content_schema.data: array of row objects: line/bar/area -> { [xKey]:string, <seriesKey>:number, ... }; pie -> { name:string, value:number }
content_schema.charts: OPTIONAL array of full chart configs to render several charts in one block

learning_beats.definition: A teaching unit one layer above blocks (setup -> model -> practice -> feedback). Mobile renders the lesson as a sequence of beats, not two panels.

auto_generation.override: If page.mobileBeatsOverride is a non-empty array, it is used verbatim.
auto_generation.single_layout: Split on headings; every activity block closes the current beat.
auto_generation.split_layout: Each right-panel activity anchors one beat; left blocks attach by heading section (if >1 heading) or by even chunking; extra left groups become trailing beats.

authoring_rules: Signpost with one heading per teaching point so beats break where meaning breaks.
Keep each right-panel activity next to (or in the same heading section as) the explanation it practices.
For a split page with N right activities, provide about N left heading sections (rough 1:1).
Order blocks setup -> model -> practice top to bottom within a panel.
One dominant activity per beat; split with a heading if a beat mixes unrelated activities.
For mobile-native interactivity prefer interaction widgets (full); treat canvas/illustration/comparison/mindmap as watch-only on phones.
